message:
fix(unistd_abi): hold ENVIRON_LOCK across forkpty() to prevent child stuck-lock

forkpty() opens master/slave PTY fds, fires the standard atfork
prepare callbacks (pthread_abi::run_atfork_prepare, membrane_state
pipeline guard), and then issues sys_clone_fork(SIGCHLD). It was
*not* taking ENVIRON_LOCK before the clone, unlike the regular
fork() path which already serializes against in-flight setenv.

Why that matters: the child returned by clone(SIGCHLD) inherits
the parent's address space copy-on-write, including any held
mutexes. The ordinary forkpty contract is that the child immediately
runs the parent-supplied setup and then the user's code (often
exec). But unlike fork+exec, forkpty children frequently keep
running rust code in-process before the eventual exec — and with
ENVIRON_LOCK potentially held by a *different* parent thread at
clone time, that lock becomes a stuck mutex in the child whose
owner thread doesn't exist on the child side. Any subsequent
getenv/setenv in the child deadlocks forever.

Fix shape mirrors fork()'s ENVIRON_LOCK acquisition exactly:

- Acquire crate::stdlib_abi::ENVIRON_LOCK after the existing pipeline
  guard but before sys_clone_fork. The lock is held only across
  the clone syscall, so the parent side's environ critical sections
  drain quickly.
- Inline comment records why we do this (forkpty child doesn't
  exec → stuck lock from another parent thread).
- On clone failure, drop _environ_guard before _pipeline_guard so
  release order matches acquire order in reverse (LIFO).
- On clone success, drop _environ_guard immediately after the
  clone returns in both parent and child contexts (the child sees
  a single-threaded snapshot, so dropping is safe and unblocks
  any subsequent setenv/getenv in the child setup path).

No new tests added in this commit — the deadlock surfaces under a
specific multi-thread setenv-race-vs-forkpty repro that requires
tooling beyond a unit test; an issue/test will follow once the
acceptance harness is available.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
